(not to be confused with [broadcast programming](/wiki/Broadcast_programming "Broadcast programming")), or **specialist station** (British English)[[1]](#cite_note-1)[[2]](#cite_note-2) describes the overall content broadcast on a radio station.[[3]](#cite_note-CEPMW-PROGRAMMING-3) The radio format emerged mainly in the United States in the 1950s, at a time when radio was compelled to develop new and exclusive ways to programming by competition with [television](/wiki/Television_broadcasting "Television broadcasting").[[4]](#cite_note-HMMUS-4) The formula has since spread as a reference for commercial radio programming worldwide.[[3]](#cite_note-CEPMW-PROGRAMMING-3)

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Overall content broadcast on a radio station

A **radio format**, **programming format** (not to be confused with [broadcast programming](/wiki/Broadcast_programming "Broadcast programming")), or **specialist station** (British English)[[1]](#cite_note-1)[[2]](#cite_note-2) describes the overall content broadcast on a radio station.[[3]](#cite_note-CEPMW-PROGRAMMING-3) The radio format emerged mainly in the United States in the 1950s, at a time when radio was compelled to develop new and exclusive ways to programming by competition with [television](/wiki/Television_broadcasting "Television broadcasting").[[4]](#cite_note-HMMUS-4) The formula has since spread as a reference for commercial radio programming worldwide.[[3]](#cite_note-CEPMW-PROGRAMMING-3)

A radio format aims to reach a more or less specific audience according to a certain type of programming, which can be thematic or general, more informative or more musical, among other possibilities.[[nb 1]](#cite_note-5) Radio formats are often used as a marketing tool and are subject to frequent changes,[[5]](#cite_note-6) including temporary changes called "[stunting](/wiki/Stunting_(broadcasting) "Stunting (broadcasting)")."

Except for [talk radio](/wiki/Talk_radio "Talk radio") or [sports radio](/wiki/Sports_radio "Sports radio") formats, most programming formats are based on commercial music.[[3]](#cite_note-CEPMW-PROGRAMMING-3) However the term also includes the news, bulletins, DJ talk, jingles, commercials, competitions, traffic news, sports, weather and community announcements between the tracks.[[3]](#cite_note-CEPMW-PROGRAMMING-3)

## Background

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Radio_format&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Background")]

Even before [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"), radio stations in North America and Europe almost always adopted a generalist radio format.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

However, the United States witnessed the growing strengthening of [television](/wiki/Television_broadcasting "Television broadcasting") over the radio as the major mass media in the country by the late 1940s.[[4]](#cite_note-HMMUS-4) [American television](/wiki/Television_in_the_United_States "Television in the United States") had more financial resources to produce generalist programs that provoked the migration of countless talents from radio networks to the new medium. Under this context, the radio was pressured to seek alternatives to maintain its audience and cultural relevance.[[4]](#cite_note-HMMUS-4)

As a consequence, [AM radios](/wiki/AM_broadcasting "AM broadcasting") stations—many of which were "independent", that is not affiliated with the network—began to emerge in the United States and Canada. They developed a format with programming consisting of music, news, and charismatic disc jockeys to directly attract a certain audience.[[4]](#cite_note-HMMUS-4)

For example, by the 1960s, [easy listening](/wiki/Easy_listening "Easy listening") obtained a stable position on [FM radio](/wiki/FM_broadcasting "FM broadcasting") – a spectrum considered ideal for good music and high fidelity listening as it grew in popularity during that period[[nb 2]](#cite_note-7)[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] – and the [middle of the road](/wiki/Middle_of_the_road_(music) "Middle of the road (music)") (MOR) rose as a radio industry term to discern radio stations that played mainstream pop songs from radio stations whose programming was geared towards teenagers and was dominated by [rock and roll](/wiki/Rock_and_roll "Rock and roll"),[[6]](#cite_note-UML-8) the most popular musical genre of the period in the United States and which held the first successful radio format called [Top-40](/wiki/Top_40 "Top 40"). In reality, the Top-40 format was conscientiously prepared to attract the young audience, who was the main consumer of the records sold by the American record industry at that time.[[4]](#cite_note-HMMUS-4) Soon, playlists became central to programming and radio formats,[[7]](#cite_note-CEPMW-PLAYLIST-9) although the number of records in a playlist really depends on the format.[[nb 3]](#cite_note-10)

By the mid-1960s, American FM radio's perception began achieving balance with AM radio since the [Federal Communications Commission](/wiki/Federal_Communications_Commission "Federal Communications Commission") required that co-owned AM and FM stations be programmed independently from each other.[[4]](#cite_note-HMMUS-4) This resulted in huge competition between radio stations in the AM and FM spectrum to differentiate themselves for both audiences and advertisers.[[6]](#cite_note-UML-8) At that time, it caused a proliferation of many radio formats, which included presentation, schedule and target audience, as well as repertoire.[[6]](#cite_note-UML-8) Within a few years, FM radio stations were supplying program formats completely analogous to their AM stations counterparts, increased to more than 50% in 1970 and reached 95% in 1980.[[6]](#cite_note-UML-8)

During the 1970s and 1980s, radio programming formats expanded into commercially successful variations, including, for example, [adult contemporary](/wiki/Adult_contemporary_music "Adult contemporary music") (AC), [album-oriented rock](/wiki/Album-oriented_rock "Album-oriented rock") (AOR) and [urban contemporary](/wiki/Urban_contemporary "Urban contemporary") (UC), among others, which spread to most AM and FM radio stations in the United States.[[4]](#cite_note-HMMUS-4)

Over time, FM radio came to dominate music programming, while AM radio switched to news and talk formats.[[8]](#cite_note-VH-BEISBIER-11)

## Regulation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Radio_format&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Regulation")]

In some countries such as the UK, licences to broadcast on radio frequencies are regulated by the government, and may take account of social and cultural factors including format type, local content, and language, as well as the price available to pay for the spectrum use. This may be done to ensure a balance of available public content in each area, and in particular to enable non-profit local community radio to exist alongside larger and richer national companies. On occasions format regulation may lead to difficult legal challenges when government accuses a station of changing its format, for example arguing in court over whether a particular song or group of songs is "pop" or "rock".[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### List of radio formats

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Radio_format&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: List of radio formats")]

### North America (United States and Canada)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Radio_format&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: North America (United States and Canada)")]

Formats constantly evolve and each format can often be sub-divided into many specialty formats. Some of the following formats are available only regionally or through specialized venues such as [satellite radio](/wiki/Satellite_radio "Satellite radio") or [Internet radio](/wiki/Internet_radio "Internet radio").[[9]](#cite_note-NY-12)

Pop/adult contemporary/rhythmic

* [Contemporary hit radio](/wiki/Contemporary_hit_radio "Contemporary hit radio") (CHR), occasionally still informally known as [top-40](/wiki/Top-40 "Top-40") / hot hits[[9]](#cite_note-NY-12))[[9]](#cite_note-NY-12)
* [Adult contemporary music](/wiki/Adult_contemporary_music "Adult contemporary music") (AC)[[9]](#cite_note-NY-12)
* [Rhythmic adult contemporary](/wiki/Rhythmic_adult_contemporary "Rhythmic adult contemporary")
* [Rhythmic contemporary](/wiki/Rhythmic_contemporary "Rhythmic contemporary") (Rhythmic Top 40)
* [Rhythmic oldies](/wiki/Rhythmic_oldies "Rhythmic oldies")
* [Adult/variety hits](/wiki/Adult_hits "Adult hits") – Broad variety of pop hits spanning multiple eras and formats; [Jack FM](/wiki/Jack_FM "Jack FM"), [Bob FM](/wiki/Bob_FM "Bob FM").
* [Classic hits](/wiki/Classic_hits "Classic hits") and [Oldies](/wiki/Oldies "Oldies") – Pop/Rock music that came between the 1950s and 2010s
* [Hot adult contemporary](/wiki/Hot_adult_contemporary "Hot adult contemporary") (Hot AC)[[9]](#cite_note-NY-12)
* [Modern adult contemporary](/wiki/Modern_adult_contemporary "Modern adult contemporary") (Modern AC)
* [Soft adult contemporary](/wiki/Soft_adult_contemporary "Soft adult contemporary") (soft AC)

Rock/alternative/indie

* [Active rock](/wiki/Active_rock "Active rock")[[9]](#cite_note-NY-12)
* [Adult album alternative](/wiki/Adult_album_alternative "Adult album alternative") (or just adult alternative) (AAA or Triple-A)[[9]](#cite_note-NY-12)
* [Album rock](/wiki/Album_rock "Album rock") / [album-oriented rock](/wiki/Album-oriented_rock "Album-oriented rock") (AOR)[[9]](#cite_note-NY-12)
* [Alternative rock](/wiki/Alternative_rock "Alternative rock")[[9]](#cite_note-NY-12)
* [Classic alternative](/wiki/Classic_alternative "Classic alternative")
* [Classic rock](/wiki/Classic_rock "Classic rock")[[9]](#cite_note-NY-12)
* [Mainstream rock](/wiki/Mainstream_rock "Mainstream rock")
* [Modern rock](/wiki/Modern_rock "Modern rock")[[9]](#cite_note-NY-12)
* [Progressive rock](/wiki/Progressive_rock_(radio_format) "Progressive rock (radio format)")
* [Psychedelic rock](/wiki/Psychedelic_rock "Psychedelic rock")
* [Soft rock](/wiki/Soft_rock "Soft rock")

Country/farm

* [Americana](/wiki/Americana_(music) "Americana (music)")[[9]](#cite_note-NY-12)
* [Bluegrass](/wiki/Bluegrass_music "Bluegrass music")
* [Country music](/wiki/Country_music "Country music"):[[9]](#cite_note-NY-12)
  + [Classic country](/wiki/Classic_country "Classic country") (exclusively older-released music)
  + New country/Young country/Hot country (top 40 country with some non-country pop and no older music)
  + [Mainstream country](/wiki/Mainstream_country "Mainstream country") (top 40 country with some older-released music)
  + [Traditional country](/wiki/Traditional_country "Traditional country") (mix of old and new-released music)
* Regional country formats: [Cajun](/wiki/Cajun_music "Cajun music") (Bayou), [Texas](/wiki/Texas_country_music "Texas country music"), [New Mexico](/wiki/New_Mexico_music "New Mexico music"), [Oklahoma Red Dirt](/wiki/Red_Dirt_(music) "Red Dirt (music)"), [Gulf and Western](/wiki/Tropical_rock "Tropical rock") (Florida), [Newfoundland](/wiki/Music_of_Newfoundland_and_Labrador "Music of Newfoundland and Labrador")

Urban/Black

* [Classic hip-hop](/wiki/Classic_hip-hop "Classic hip-hop")
* [Quiet storm](/wiki/Quiet_storm "Quiet storm") (most often a "daypart" late night format at urban and urban AC stations, i.e. 7 p.m.–12 midnight)
* Urban:[[9]](#cite_note-NY-12)
  + [Urban contemporary](/wiki/Urban_contemporary "Urban contemporary") (mostly [rap](/wiki/Hip_hop_music "Hip hop music"), [hip hop](/wiki/Hip_hop_music "Hip hop music"), [soul](/wiki/Soul_music "Soul music"), and [contemporary R&B](/wiki/Contemporary_R%26B "Contemporary R&B") artists)
  + [Urban adult contemporary](/wiki/Urban_adult_contemporary "Urban adult contemporary") (Urban AC)[[9]](#cite_note-NY-12) – R&B (both released [newer](/wiki/Contemporary_R%26B "Contemporary R&B") and [older](/wiki/Rhythm_and_blues "Rhythm and blues")), [soul](/wiki/Soul_music "Soul music") and sometimes [gospel music](/wiki/Gospel_music "Gospel music"), without [hip hop](/wiki/Hip_hop_music "Hip hop music") and [rap](/wiki/Hip_hop_music "Hip hop music")
  + [Urban oldies](/wiki/Urban_oldies "Urban oldies") (sometimes called "[classic soul](/wiki/Classic_soul "Classic soul")", "R&B [oldies](/wiki/Oldies "Oldies")", or "[old school](/wiki/Urban_oldies "Urban oldies")")
* [Soul music](/wiki/Soul_music "Soul music")

Dance/electronic

* Dance (dance top-40)[[9]](#cite_note-NY-12)
* [Space music](/wiki/Space_music "Space music")
* [Carolina beach music](/wiki/Beach_music "Beach music") (regional in the Carolinas; mostly R&B and some pop-country with shuffle beat)

Jazz/blues/standards

* [Big band](/wiki/Big_band "Big band")[[9]](#cite_note-NY-12)
* [Blues](/wiki/Blues "Blues")
* [Jazz](/wiki/Jazz "Jazz")[[9]](#cite_note-NY-12)
* [Smooth jazz](/wiki/Smooth_jazz "Smooth jazz")[[9]](#cite_note-NY-12)
* [Traditional pop](/wiki/Traditional_pop "Traditional pop")

Easy listening/New Age

* [Adult standards](/wiki/Adult_standards "Adult standards") / nostalgia (pre-rock)[[9]](#cite_note-NY-12)
* [Beautiful music](/wiki/Beautiful_music "Beautiful music")
* [Easy listening](/wiki/Easy_listening "Easy listening")
* [Middle of the road](/wiki/Middle_of_the_road_(music) "Middle of the road (music)") (MOR)

Folk/singer-songwriters

* Folk music

Latin

* Spanish-language rhythmic
* [Ranchera](/wiki/Ranchera "Ranchera")
* [Regional Mexican](/wiki/Regional_Mexican "Regional Mexican") ([Banda](/wiki/Banda_music "Banda music"), [mariachi](/wiki/Mariachi "Mariachi"), [norteño](/wiki/Norte%C3%B1o_(music) "Norteño (music)"), etc.)
* [Rock en Español](/wiki/Rock_en_Espa%C3%B1ol "Rock en Español")
* Romántica (Spanish-language AC)
* Spanish-language sub-formats:[[9]](#cite_note-NY-12)
  + [Tejano music](/wiki/Tejano_music "Tejano music") ([Texas](/wiki/Texas "Texas")/Mexican music)
  + Also see: [Ranchera](/wiki/Ranchera "Ranchera"), [Regional Mexican](/wiki/Regional_Mexican "Regional Mexican"), Romántica, and [Tropical](/wiki/Tropical_(radio_format) "Tropical (radio format)")
* [Tropical](/wiki/Tropical_(radio_format) "Tropical (radio format)") (salsa, merengue, cumbia, etc.)
* [Urbano](/wiki/Urbano_music "Urbano music") (reggaetón, Latin rap, etc.)

Multicultural

* [Caribbean](/wiki/Caribbean_music "Caribbean music") ([reggae](/wiki/Reggae "Reggae"), [soca](/wiki/Soca_music "Soca music"), [merengue](/wiki/Merengue_music "Merengue music"), [cumbia](/wiki/Cumbia "Cumbia"), [salsa](/wiki/Salsa_music "Salsa music"), etc.)
* Desi music
* [Asian pop](/wiki/Music_of_Asia "Music of Asia")
  + [Japanese music](/wiki/Japanese_music "Japanese music") ([J-pop](/wiki/Japanese_pop "Japanese pop"), [J-rock](/wiki/Japanese_rock "Japanese rock"), [Anisong](/wiki/Anisong "Anisong"), [city pop](/wiki/City_pop "City pop"), etc.)
  + [Korean music](/wiki/Korean_music "Korean music") ([K-pop](/wiki/Korean_pop "Korean pop"), [K-rock](/wiki/Korean_rock "Korean rock"), etc.)
  + [Traditional Philippine music](/wiki/Music_of_the_Philippines "Music of the Philippines")
* [Polka](/wiki/Polka "Polka")
* Tribal/Native
* [World music](/wiki/World_music "World music")[[9]](#cite_note-NY-12)

Faith-based

* [Christian music](/wiki/Christian_music "Christian music")
  + [Contemporary Christian music](/wiki/Contemporary_Christian_music "Contemporary Christian music") (CCM)
  + [Christian adult contemporary](/wiki/Christian_adult_contemporary "Christian adult contemporary")
  + [Christian CHR](/wiki/Christian_contemporary_hit_radio "Christian contemporary hit radio")
  + [Contemporary worship music](/wiki/Contemporary_worship_music "Contemporary worship music")
  + [Urban contemporary gospel](/wiki/Urban_contemporary_gospel "Urban contemporary gospel")
  + [Southern Gospel](/wiki/Southern_Gospel "Southern Gospel")
  + Traditional [hymns](/wiki/Hymn "Hymn") (e.g. [Bible Broadcasting Network](/wiki/Bible_Broadcasting_Network "Bible Broadcasting Network"), [Fundamental Broadcasting Network](/wiki/Fundamental_Broadcasting_Network "Fundamental Broadcasting Network"))

Classical

* [Classical](/wiki/Western_classical_music "Western classical music")[[9]](#cite_note-NY-12)
* [Contemporary classical music](/wiki/Contemporary_classical_music "Contemporary classical music")

Seasonal/holiday/events

Seasonal formats typically celebrate a particular holiday and thus, with the notable exception of Christmas music (which is usually played throughout [Advent](/wiki/Advent "Advent")), stations going to a holiday-themed format usually only do so for a short time, typically a day or a weekend.

* [Christmas music radio](/wiki/Christmas_music_radio "Christmas music radio") (usually seasonal, mainly late November into December)
* [American patriotic music](/wiki/American_patriotic_music "American patriotic music") (short-term format, usually adopted around holidays such as [Fourth of July](/wiki/Independence_Day_(United_States) "Independence Day (United States)") and [Memorial Day](/wiki/Memorial_Day_(United_States) "Memorial Day (United States)"))
  + In Canada, the equivalent of this is an all-[CanCon](/wiki/Canadian_content "Canadian content") format[[10]](#cite_note-13) for [Canada Day](/wiki/Canada_Day "Canada Day") or [Victoria Day](/wiki/Victoria_Day "Victoria Day")
* Halloween music (usually only on or around 31 October)
* [Irish folk music](/wiki/Irish_folk_music "Irish folk music") (usually only on or around 17 March to celebrate [Saint Patrick's Day](/wiki/Saint_Patrick%27s_Day "Saint Patrick's Day"))
* [Summer music](/wiki/Summer_hit "Summer hit") (June to August in the Northern Hemisphere)

Miscellanies

* [Eclectic](/wiki/Eclecticism_in_music "Eclecticism in music")
* [Freeform radio](/wiki/Freeform_radio "Freeform radio") (DJ-selected)

Spoken word formats

* [All-news radio](/wiki/All-news_radio "All-news radio")
* [Children's](/wiki/Children%27s_radio "Children's radio")
* [Christian radio](/wiki/Christian_radio "Christian radio")
* [College radio](/wiki/College_radio "College radio")
* [Comedy radio](/wiki/Comedy_radio "Comedy radio")
* Educational
* [Ethnic](/wiki/Ethnic "Ethnic")/International[[9]](#cite_note-NY-12)
* [Freeform/Experimental](/wiki/Freeform_(radio_format) "Freeform (radio format)")
* [Full-service](/wiki/Full_service_(radio_format) "Full service (radio format)") (talk and variety music)
* [Old time radio](/wiki/Old_time_radio "Old time radio")
* [Paranormal radio shows](/wiki/Paranormal_radio_shows "Paranormal radio shows")
* [Radio audiobooks](/wiki/Radio_audiobooks "Radio audiobooks") (see also [radio reading service](/wiki/Radio_reading_service "Radio reading service"))
* Radio documentary
* [Radio drama](/wiki/Radio_drama "Radio drama")
  + [Radio soap operas](/wiki/List_of_radio_soaps "List of radio soaps")
* [Religious radio](/wiki/Religious_radio "Religious radio")
* [Sports](/wiki/Sports_radio "Sports radio") (Sports talk)
* [News/Talk](/wiki/News/Talk "News/Talk")
  + [Conservative talk radio](/wiki/Conservative_talk_radio "Conservative talk radio")
  + [Progressive talk radio](/wiki/Progressive_talk_radio "Progressive talk radio")
  + [Public talk radio](/wiki/Public_radio "Public radio")
  + Hot talk/[shock jocks](/wiki/Shock_jock "Shock jock")
* [Weather radio](/wiki/Weather_radio "Weather radio")

### United Kingdom

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Radio_format&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: United Kingdom")]

Music-oriented

The UK has several formats that often overlap with one another. The American terms for formats are not always used to describe British stations or fully set specified by [RAJAR](/wiki/RAJAR "RAJAR").[[11]](#cite_note-14)

* [Contemporary hit radio](/wiki/Contemporary_hit_radio "Contemporary hit radio") (CHR) / Top 40 - e.g. [Capital](/wiki/Capital_(radio_network) "Capital (radio network)"), [BBC Radio 1](/wiki/BBC_Radio_1 "BBC Radio 1"), [Hits Radio](/wiki/Hits_Radio_UK "Hits Radio UK")
  + [Rhythmic contemporary](/wiki/Rhythmic_contemporary "Rhythmic contemporary") (Rhythmic CHR) - e.g. [Kiss](/wiki/Kiss_(UK_radio_station) "Kiss (UK radio station)"), [Capital Xtra](/wiki/Capital_Xtra "Capital Xtra")
* [Adult contemporary music](/wiki/Adult_contemporary_music "Adult contemporary music") (AC) – e.g. [BBC Radio 2](/wiki/BBC_Radio_2 "BBC Radio 2"), [Magic](/wiki/Magic_Radio "Magic Radio")
  + Hot adult contemporary (hot AC), middle ground between CHR and soft AC – e.g. [Heart](/wiki/Heart_Radio "Heart Radio"),[[12]](#cite_note-15) [Virgin Radio](/wiki/Virgin_Radio "Virgin Radio")
  + Soft adult contemporary (soft AC) – e.g. [Smooth Radio](/wiki/Smooth_Radio_(2014) "Smooth Radio (2014)")[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]
  + [Urban/black adult contemporary](/wiki/Urban_adult_contemporary "Urban adult contemporary") with stronger leaning to urban and hip-hop – e.g. [BBC Radio 1Xtra](/wiki/BBC_Radio_1Xtra "BBC Radio 1Xtra")[[13]](#cite_note-16)
  + Modern adult contemporary (modern AC) with stronger leaning to rock – e.g. [Absolute Radio](/wiki/Absolute_Radio "Absolute Radio")
* [Oldies](/wiki/Oldies "Oldies"), which can range from the 1950s to the 2000s depending on the station – e.g. [Gold](/wiki/Gold_(British_radio_network) "Gold (British radio network)"), [Greatest Hits Radio](/wiki/Greatest_Hits_Radio "Greatest Hits Radio"), [Kisstory](/wiki/Kisstory "Kisstory"), [Absolute 80s](/wiki/Absolute_80s "Absolute 80s")
* Alternative, sometimes confused with rock – e.g. [Radio X](/wiki/Radio_X_(United_Kingdom) "Radio X (United Kingdom)"), [BBC Radio 6 Music](/wiki/BBC_Radio_6_Music "BBC Radio 6 Music")
* Classical – e.g. [Classic FM](/wiki/Classic_FM_(UK) "Classic FM (UK)"), [BBC Radio 3](/wiki/BBC_Radio_3 "BBC Radio 3")
* Asian, referring to content for British Asian communities (including Indian, Pakistani, Sri Lankan, Bangladeshi, Afghan, Nepalese)[[14]](#cite_note-17)[[15]](#cite_note-18) – e.g. [BBC Asian Network](/wiki/BBC_Asian_Network "BBC Asian Network"), [Sunrise Radio](/wiki/Sunrise_Radio "Sunrise Radio")
* Ethnic, referring to content for specific ethnic communities – e.g. [London Greek Radio](/wiki/London_Greek_Radio "London Greek Radio"),[[16]](#cite_note-19) [Spectrum Radio](/wiki/Spectrum_Radio "Spectrum Radio")
* [Community](/wiki/Community_radio_in_the_United_Kingdom "Community radio in the United Kingdom"), radio stations for localised communities which can be in various formats including ethnic

Spoken-words

* News and talk – e.g. [LBC](/wiki/LBC "LBC"), [BBC Radio 5 Live](/wiki/BBC_Radio_5_Live "BBC Radio 5 Live"), [Times Radio](/wiki/Times_Radio "Times Radio")
  + Sports broadcasting and talk – e.g. [BBC Radio 5 Live](/wiki/BBC_Radio_5_Live "BBC Radio 5 Live"), [Talksport](/wiki/Talksport "Talksport")
* News, talk, documentaries and drama – e.g. [BBC Radio 4](/wiki/BBC_Radio_4 "BBC Radio 4")
* Christian, broadcasts for Christian religious interests with occasional music – e.g. [Premier Christian Radio](/wiki/Premier_Christian_Radio "Premier Christian Radio")

## Notes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Radio_format&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Notes")]

1. **[^](#cite_ref-5)** Music radio, old time radio, all-news radio, sports radio, talk radio and weather radio describe the operation of different genres of radio format and each format can often be sub-divided into many specialty formats.
2. **[^](#cite_ref-7)** At that time, there were several American FM stations that belonged to owners of AM stations, so the programming of the AM station was broadcast simultaneously with the station FM. Owners who programmed FM stations independently often did so using avant-garde, underground, jazz or highbrow (generally, classical music) program formats as a form to attract the few listeners who owned FM receivers and who were specific about signal quality they heard.[[4]](#cite_note-HMMUS-4)
3. **[^](#cite_ref-10)** The figure 40 was established by Todd Storz and Bill Stewart n their station KOWH-AM in Omaha, Nebraska, inspired by the fact that there were 40 records in a bar jukebox. In the 1960s, some radio formats reduced the figure to 30 records, or even just 10.[[7]](#cite_note-CEPMW-PLAYLIST-9)
